records.ety: [OE. maimen, OF. mahaignier, mehaignier, meshaignier, cf. It. magagnare, LL. mahemiare, mahennare; perh. of Celtic origin; cf. Armor. mac'haña to mutilate, māc'ha to crowd, press; or cf. OHG. mangōn to lack, perh. akin to E. mangle to lacerate. Cf. Mayhem.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Maimed (māmd);p. pr. & vb. n. Maiming.]

defs: To deprive of the use of a limb, so as to render a person in fighting less able either to defend himself or to annoy his adversary.
quotes: By the ancient law of England he that maimed any man whereby he lost any part of his body, was sentenced to lose the like part.

defs: To mutilate; to cripple; to injure; to disable; to impair.
quotes: My late maimed limbs lack wonted might.
You maimed the jurisdiction of all bishops.

defs: The privation of the use of a limb or member of the body, by which one is rendered less able to defend himself or to annoy his adversary.
The privation of any necessary part; a crippling; mutilation; injury; deprivation of something essential. See Mayhem.
quotes: Surely there is more cause to fear lest the want there of be a maim than the use of it a blemish.
A noble author esteems it to be a maim in history that the acts of Parliament should not be recited.
